serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh loquat is approximately 3 to 4 medium fruits (about 100g), consumed raw as a snack or dessert fruit.

nutrient_source.title: Loquat, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 786711)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw loquat used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB database values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, and iron are consistent with USDA data and are preserved from the input record. Sugar and saturated fat values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Loquat — analogous estimate based on low-GI stone and pome fruits; closest peer-reviewed reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for loquat is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 46 is estimated based on loquat's carbohydrate composition (12.14g/100g, ~7.5g sugars, 1.7g fibre), its botanical classification as a pome fruit (Rosaceae family), and comparison with similarly structured low-GI fruits (peach GI ~42, plum GI ~40, apple GI ~38–44) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 100g serving = (46 × 12.14) / 100 = 5.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1mg/100g) and high potassium (266mg/100g) supporting DASH dietary pattern
hypertension.guidance: Loquat's near-zero sodium and high potassium content make it an excellent fruit choice for hypertension management; consume fresh (not canned in syrup) in moderate portions of 100–150g to maximise the sodium-to-potassium benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~46) with 1.7g dietary fibre moderating glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Loquat's low glycaemic index (~46) and dietary fibre content slow postprandial glucose rise; limit portions to 100–150g per serving to manage total carbohydrate intake (~12g/100g), and avoid canned loquat in syrup which significantly increases sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium with antioxidant beta-carotene
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Loquat's very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium (266mg/100g), and beta-carotene (76mcg RAE/100g) support cardiovascular health; it is suitable as a regular fruit component of a heart-healthy diet in standard fresh-fruit portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant beta-carotene and polyphenols present but no direct omega-3 or strong anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Loquat provides beta-carotene and polyphenolic antioxidants that may support anti-inflammatory pathways, but lacks omega-3 fatty acids and has only 1.7g fibre per 100g; it is a neutral-to-mildly beneficial fruit for RA when consumed fresh as part of a varied anti-inflammatory diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Loquat (Eriobotrya japonica) is a small, golden-orange pome fruit native to south-central China and widely cultivated across East Asia, the Mediterranean, and subtropical regions worldwide. The fruit has a smooth to slightly downy skin, tender juicy flesh ranging from pale yellow to orange, and a sweet-tart flavour profile reminiscent of a cross between peach, apricot, and mild citrus. Each fruit contains one to several large brown seeds. Loquats are consumed fresh as a snack or dessert fruit, and are also used in jams, jellies, syrups, and traditional Chinese herbal preparations. Nutritionally, loquat provides approximately 47 kcal per 100g with 12.14g carbohydrates (of which ~7.5g are natural sugars), 0.43g protein, 0.2g fat, and 1.7g dietary fibre. It is notably rich in potassium (266mg/100g) and beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor, ~76mcg RAE/100g), and has near-zero sodium (1mg/100g). Its estimated glycaemic index of approximately 46 places it in the low-GI category, making it a suitable fruit for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio aligns with DASH dietary recommendations for blood pressure management. Loquat is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is widely available in Singapore and Southeast Asia during its seasonal harvest.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of loquat?
Is loquat good for blood pressure?
Is loquat the same as Japanese plum?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw loquat (FDC ID 786711). Secondary nutrients (sugar, saturated fat, vitamins, minerals) sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as no direct loquat GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; this is clearly documented.
notes: 1. Sugar_g value of 7.5g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw loquat and is consistent with the input record's estimate. 2. Saturated_fat_g (0.04g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.008g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.091g), and trans_fat_g (0.0g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA data for raw loquat. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 as loquat is a plant food and these nutrients are naturally absent; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. GI of ~46 is an analogous estimate; no direct loquat GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Estimation basis: comparison with Rosaceae pome and drupe fruits (peach GI ~42, plum GI ~40) per Atkinson et al. 2008. 5. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible best-match entries; FoodEx2 code A00LC is the standard FoodEx2 entry for loquat in the EFSA FoodEx2 system. Reviewers should verify FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code against current ontology releases. 6. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as loquat is a plant food with no dietary cholesterol (naturally zero per USDA SR Legacy). 7. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all -1 fields in the template have been replaced with sourced numeric values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
